chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Asynchronous JavaScript is one of the most important topics you will learn.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Synchronous code runs line by line. Each line waits for the previous one to finish.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Asynchronous code can run in the background without blocking the main thread.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: This is important because web browsers are single-threaded.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: If a long operation blocks the main thread, the entire page becomes unresponsive.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: setTimeout is a simple example of asynchronous code. It runs a function after a delay.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: setInterval runs a function repeatedly at specified intervals.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Callbacks are functions passed to other functions to be called after an operation completes.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Callback hell or pyramid of doom happens when you nest too many callbacks.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Promises were introduced to solve the callback hell problem.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: A Promise represents a value that may be available now, in the future, or never.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: A Promise can be in one of three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: You create a Promise using new Promise with a function that takes resolve and reject.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: The then method is called when a Promise is fulfilled. Catch is called when it is rejected.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Promise chaining allows you to chain multiple then calls for sequential operations.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Promise.all takes an array of promises and resolves when all of them are fulfilled.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Promise.race resolves or rejects as soon as one of the promises resolves or rejects.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Async await is syntactic sugar over Promises that makes async code look synchronous.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: The async keyword before a function makes it return a Promise automatically.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: The await keyword pauses the execution until the Promise is resolved.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: You can only use await inside an async function.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Error handling in async await is done using try-catch blocks.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: The fetch API is used to make HTTP requests and returns a Promise.

chunks.title: Async JavaScript - Callbacks, Promises & Async Await
chunks.text: Understanding async JavaScript is crucial for working with APIs and servers.

text: Asynchronous JavaScript is one of the most important topics you will learn. Synchronous code runs line by line. Each line waits for the previous one to finish. Asynchronous code can run in the background without blocking the main thread. This is important because web browsers are single-threaded. If a long operation blocks the main thread, the entire page becomes unresponsive. setTimeout is a simple example of asynchronous code. It runs a function after a delay. setInterval runs a function repeatedly at specified intervals. Callbacks are functions passed to other functions to be called after an operation completes. Callback hell or pyramid of doom happens when you nest too many callbacks. Promises were introduced to solve the callback hell problem. A Promise represents a value that may be available now, in the future, or never. A Promise can be in one of three states: pending, fulfilled, or rejected. You create a Promise using new Promise with a function that takes resolve and reject. The then method is called when a Promise is fulfilled. Catch is called when it is rejected. Promise chaining allows you to chain multiple then calls for sequential operations. Promise.all takes an array of promises and resolves when all of them are fulfilled. Promise.race resolves or rejects as soon as one of the promises resolves or rejects. Async await is syntactic sugar over Promises that makes async code look synchronous. The async keyword before a function makes it return a Promise automatically. The await keyword pauses the execution until the Promise is resolved. You can only use await inside an async function. Error handling in async await is done using try-catch blocks. The fetch API is used to make HTTP requests and returns a Promise. Understanding async JavaScript is crucial for working with APIs and servers.
